source_intake_record.raw_question_text: he focal length of a concave lens can be measured using a converging lens and a plane mirror. The method is as follows: First, let a point light source $Q$ form a real image at point $Q'$ through the concave lens. Then, place the concave lens to be measured between the concave lens and $Q'$. The concave lens and the concave lens are coaxial, and a plane mirror is placed on the right side of the concave lens, along the optical axis, as shown in Figure. Move the concave lens to a suitable position, so that $Q$ is superimposed on its own image formed by the entire optical system. Measure the distance between the concave lens and the concave lens. Calculate the focal length of the concave lens.

This is not simple lookup; it combines multiple conditions from the apparatus description and likely the figure.
There are 4 answer choices with symbolic expressions, suggesting derivation/comparison rather than direct extraction.
verifiability: The expected answer is a short symbolic expression, and the item also provides 4 fixed choices, making final-answer checking feasible.

asset_records.normalized_text_snapshot:
The focal length of a concave lens can be measured using another lens and a plane mirror. A point light source Q first forms a real image at point Q'. Then the concave lens to be measured is placed between the other lens and Q'. The two lenses are coaxial, and a plane mirror is placed on the right side of the measured concave lens along the optical axis, as shown in the figure. Move the measured concave lens to a suitable position so that Q coincides with its own image formed by the entire optical system. If the measured distance between the two lenses is s and the relevant distance shown in the setup is d, what is the focal length of the concave lens?
(Express your answer as a formula in terms of d and s.)
